title: Performance and Optimization
summary: Profile, identify bottlenecks, and optimize Python code using profiling tools and best practices

sections.title: Don't guess — measure
sections.context: Optimizing without data is like trying to fix a car engine by randomly replacing parts. You might make things worse. The first rule of optimization is 'measure, don't guess.' Python provides profiling tools: cProfile for function-level timing, timeit for micro-benchmarks, and line_profiler for line-by-line analysis. Always profile before optimizing to identify the actual bottleneck, which often surprises developers — it's rarely where you expect. Premature optimization (optimizing before you have data) is the root of much evil. Write clean, correct code first. Then profile. Then optimize only the critical 20% that accounts for 80% of the runtime.

sections.title: Using cProfile and timeit to measure performance
sections.content: Profile two approaches to find the slowest part of the code
sections.code:
import timeit  # For precise micro-benchmarks of small code snippets
import cProfile  # For function-level profiling of larger programs
import pstats  # For sorting and displaying profile results

def slow_approach(items):
    # Inefficient: builds a new list by concatenating in a loop (O(n^2))
    result = []  # Start with empty list
    for i in items:  # Iterate over each item
        result = result + [i]  # Concatenation creates a NEW list each time!
    return result

def fast_approach(items):
    # Efficient: list comprehension builds in one pass (O(n))
    return [i for i in items]  # Single allocation, no repeated copying

# timeit measures how long a snippet takes, running it many times
slow_time = timeit.timeit(
    'slow_approach(range(1000))',  # Code string to measure
    globals=globals(),  # Make our functions available
    number=100  # Run 100 times and return total duration
)

fast_time = timeit.timeit(
    'fast_approach(range(1000))',
    globals=globals(),
    number=100
)

print(f"Slow: {slow_time:.4f}s")
print(f"Fast: {fast_time:.4f}s")
print(f"Speedup: {slow_time / fast_time:.1f}x")

# For larger programs, use cProfile:
# cProfile.run('my_function()', 'profile_output')
# p = pstats.Stats('profile_output')
# p.sort_stats('cumtime').print_stats(10)  # Top 10 by cumulative time

sections.title: Common optimization strategies
sections.context: After profiling, target these common patterns. Use local variable bindings: accessing a local variable is faster than a global or attribute access — cache module-level functions in local scope inside hot loops. Use built-in functions and libraries: Python's built-in functions (map, filter, sum, any, all) are implemented in C and run much faster than manual loops. Consider NumPy for numerical operations — orders of magnitude faster than pure Python lists. Use generators for large sequences to avoid memory exhaustion. Use collections.deque instead of list for stack/queue operations (O(1) append/pop vs O(n) for list.pop(0)). Use set for membership tests instead of lists (O(1) vs O(n)).

sections.title: Micro-optimizations are usually not worth it
sections.context: Spending hours optimizing a function that runs 0.1% of total execution time is a waste. Profile first — you'll likely find the bottleneck is somewhere you didn't expect (often I/O, network, or database queries). Wrong: obsessing over x**2 vs x*x or list append vs list comprehension in non-hot code. Correct: optimize algorithms (e.g., O(n^2) to O(n log n)), add caching (functools.lru_cache), reduce I/O, or use vectorized operations. A 10x algorithm improvement beats a 10% micro-optimization every time.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Take a slow list operation: generate a list of 10,000 random integers, then filter and transform them (keep only even numbers, multiply each by 3). Write four versions: (1) a for loop with .append(), (2) a list comprehension, (3) map/filter, (4) a generator expression. Use timeit to benchmark each version with 1000 runs. Print the results and order them from fastest to slowest.
hints: How do you use timeit to measure just the execution time of a function?
Which of the four approaches do you expect to be fastest — and why?
